speaker: interviewee
text: The story that I want.

speaker: interviewer
text: 30 Minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: 30 Minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another 30 minutes. Then I'm gonna, Mama, I'm going to say good-bye to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Faygeh, let's back up because there's some stories about your family that I'd like to include. Would you tell us about your-- your grandfather-- your father's activities. When-- when [INAUDIBLE]-- no, when [? Horadot ?] was burning, what did he do with the scrolls?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took out-- in our house, my father took in our-- the minyan. This meant the-- the scroll and he took care. He was the-- you know, like a-- like a rabbi, like a cantor. He-- he's conducting the-- the prayers. And when-- and when this happened, when they bombed, he took out the scroll and he run to save the-- the scroll and save himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened? We have to move to Maniewicze, because all the houses were-- was burned. And in Maniewicze, we-- we find a place to live. And we stay and the Nazis came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, we live in fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but the family was together?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. At what point-- at what point did your husband go to the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to the forest because he heard they're gonna take to work men. So he said, they're not gonna take me, any event. And a good Ukraine person gave him a rifle and two hand grenades and bullets. And-- and he has a connection. There is some-- a group. Jewish people in Ukraine are in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he joined them?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- he joined. Yeah. And we stayed there for two year-- for two years in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that-- did you know where he was? Did you know that he was even alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that he is somewhere alive. And I mean, I was-- two young people came to find out if someone is alive, and they told me that my husband is-- is in-- in the group, partisans, and then they-- they take me along and I went with this-- with this young man to-- to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's-- before we-- before we discuss the partisans in the forest, I'd like to clarify the actions that were taken in the ghetto. The first one was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the ghetto was very short, very short. Very short time, the ghetto. Because the, you know, the Nazis saw that the people during-- during the ghetto, during the gathering and a lot of people run out. So they-- they surround us all around and put us in one ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- was the ghetto surrounded with a fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did they keep you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was no surrounding with the-- with the fence. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. A few were Ukraine. People and one Nazi and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in-- in 1941, there was an [INAUDIBLE] and--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, They took all the men away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except were there some members--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the women-- the women and children and old-- in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did they also take your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1941, did they take your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, my father was hidden in a garage. So he was alive till 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that year, how-- how did you-- did your father conduct services? Did you have enough men for a minyan?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There's not enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you do throughout that year from 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what happened in September of 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us out, and they put us in the middle of the street. And they counted all the people, and they took us over to our-- this Maniewicze was divided in the middle. This city, Maniewicze was the railroad. So they took us from one place to the other place and put us in a ghetto. And this was on a Friday. And-- and on a Saturday, they took out everybody to-- to kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a Saturday? Where did they take them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same place.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you with that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took them to the field and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what happened. They-- they shot them. Killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through a miracle, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go then? [? SON: ?] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I go? Oh, I went-- in-- in this ghetto, I went to visit my cousin and-- and his family. They live in this-- this place in the ghetto where they put us in this-- in this ghetto, in this street. So I visit my cousin, and they-- they are not in the house. And I-- and I went into a barn and I was sitting in this barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, I saw my cousin came out from this hiding place, and she went into the house. So that time, I came out and I says, where are you hiding? Take me along. So she took me on-- on the-- on the attic of-- of this house. And then in the next morning, we-- we went down, was quiet and we crawl-- we-- through the railroad and we went in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one Polish person whose name was Slowek. He took in all who survived, who run out. He took me. He was a guard. He guard the forest, a private forest, and he took all the people to this forest, and he took care on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a Polish person.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish person. Very special person. Good man.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my-- in this forest, I told you before that two or three boys came to find out what-- what happened. And-- and-- and I went to them, and I met my husband in the-- in the forest with the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he surprised to see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to tell him what had happened at Maniewicze?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew already.

speaker: interviewer
text: He knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would he have found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About what happened at Maniewicze?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a connection with the Ukrainian people. Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of your Ukrainian neighbors to what was going on at Maniewicze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, they're anti-Semite and some not.

speaker: interviewer
text: The group that you joined, the partisan group that you joined in the forest, was it a Jewish group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it Jewish? Mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a mix.

speaker: interviewee
text: More Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: More Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then we have more-- you know, the Russian army, which they cannot go through the borderline, they went into the forest. So we have a Russian headquarters. We have groups every 10 kilometer a group, not together. We didn't stay together. You know, in case the general come-- one group to, you know, to kill or to surround us. So that's why they divided in groups the people. 10 kilometer a group, 100 people or 50 people in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you select who would belong in each group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't select. The commander select.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any-- any ideas of what-- who we wanted in each group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. There was a group, men, which they are fighters and the civilian was separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the cook. You cooked. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: For 100 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: For 100 people. Could-- can you describe the commanders or any outstanding personalities in this partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- our commander was a Ukraine. His name was Kruk, [? Gunishtuk ?] Kruk. He was a Communist during the Polish regime. So when the war started, he run out. He came to Kiev and in Kiev, they gathered all more together the army and more and-- and they send him back, you know, the-- the officers, you know, the general or-- They said-- they said you have to go back to your town and organize our partisans in the forest. And this you can help us underground to do underground work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Kruk himself Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a good commander. He take good care of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Kruk group, how many totally do you-- do you estimate how many partisans were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- about 200.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was broken into about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Interim groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: Covering how much territory?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 kilometers apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 kilometers apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about how much territory would this have covered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the forest was large.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Children were in-- in civilian camp, separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Separate from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Women, children, elderly people.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were elderly people also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell what types of-- how did they divide the responsibilities? I mean, you cooked. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Responsibilities for the-- for the men?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: From beginning, they went at night burn bridges, destroyed the electricity. From beginning. And after a while, the headquarters send in a-- a very important person who knew how to make bombs. So when the war started, this man, his name was-- I forgot. I forgot already.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Milanek. ?] Was it [? Milanek? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Something. Makhamed. Makhamed. So when the war started, he was working with the cannons. So he hid the big bullets in the ground, this man. And when-- when he came to us, he says, we'll go, we'll dig out those bullets, we'll bring them to the forest, and we'll try to make bomb. What they did, they put-- unscrew the capsule, put them in kettles, in water, put fire under till the toll melted. You know, the toll-- the-- the-- And when this was melted, they made little boxes-- wooden boxes and attach with all kind, you know, batteries and-- and they put this toll in these boxes and this was the-- the bomb. And with those bomb, they went on the railroad at night, under the tracks, they put under the tracks. And when the-- the train from Germany went every hour on the hour with ammunition, with soldiers, with food. and-- and the partisan put those bomb under the tracks, and-- and they explode.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you from beginning, we have to go at night and find food. And then after a while, the Ukrainian people support us. Yes. And one night I saw two airplanes threw a lot of things like clothes, some kind of food, you know, the Communist. And we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember anything, any of the meals you cooked? What-- what-- what did they like to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes in the morning, potatoes at night. Twice a day I cook potatoes. Potatoes. We didn't have meat. We didn't-- from beginning, just potatoes, but no salt. Potatoes and water and that's all. From beginning, we-- we make fires and bake potatoes. This was our food. We didn't have bread, just potatoes. Very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- how were you housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have a tent? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We made the-- yes, from logs. You put like this and this-- and this was our-- they called the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, tent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with other partisan groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other partisan groups were there in this forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was groups-- a lot of groups and all kind commanders, Russians. Russian commanders. And we have the headquarters, you know, they used to come every evening to tell us what is going on on the front. They have radios too. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was discipline maintained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very disciplined. Fine. The best. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do it? Supposing people didn't obey orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were not much discipline. This is to take care of the guards. Some people, you know, to change guards every two hours. You know, and this group has to wash, the young ladies, they wash clothes and was divided. Was a-- good divided.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the forest then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years. And-- and how did you manage when it was very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very cold? It was not too cold in the forest. From beginning we made fire outside and-- and around the fire. And then we have the-- those tents and-- and we find by Ukraine people those iron [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wood. You-- we survived. For the-- for the cold weather, we-- we were not afraid. We're afraid only for the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you use any other name while you were a partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called me [? Saria. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The commander. Because Faygeh is more Jewish. So they called me Faygeh. The commander gave us the names. And my husband's name was [? Zelenko. ?] [? Zelenko ?] is-- [? zelano ?] is green. Forest is green. They called him [? Zelenko. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were [? Saria. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. And you continued this kind of activity for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated and by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Red Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you-- when you were liberated by the Red Army, reveal your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your-- was your partisan group recognized for its actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. By the-- because our commander, he had a diary. And this diary he made, they took us to Moscow. And-- and then my cousin find this book in Russian language. He translate it into Hebrew and we translated into English. And-- and my son, Jack, he's a writer. So he wrote a lot of books.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they find this diary?

speaker: interviewee
text: They find it in-- in Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the year about when they found it, the year they found it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was found already after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, this is the book. You saw?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw this book. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated and then what happened after you were liberated by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: After war, you're on you're own. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the bigger cities. We went to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne. Rowne is a big city. And there we find an apartment, one-room apartment. But was-- our houses was bombed. Destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: They destroy in 1944. They bombed the city for one-- for one year. And they-- they-- it's hard to-- was hard to find a place to live. So we find one-room apartment in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944. So my son, Jack, was born in Rowne. I was pregnant in-- under the bombs all the time, the nine months. And-- and when the time came to-- [INAUDIBLE] to have my baby, my husband put me in a carriage, a horse and wagon. He took me to a hospital. There was no heat and no light. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. And I survived with the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and-- what-- what else I want to tell you? And this one-room apartment, we took in two orphans. The father was killed in-- in the army, in the war, during the war. The mother was freeze to death in the forest. She went to look for food and she couldn't-- she got lost. So we took on those two children, a boy and a girl, sister and brother, and they stayed with us till we came up to Russia. We came up to Russia. We stopped in Boynton, Germany. There the [? bruha ?] in [? Nagana, ?] they gathered all the orphaned and they went to Israel. The children?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the children. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you and your husband go and Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the baby, we start-- you know, we told you. We plan to go to Palestine. And we couldn't go through because they didn't give us certificate to go through. And we stopped. We travel, travel all over. And we stopped in Austria. Bindermichl was a camp, displace camp. And there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bindermichl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bindermichl. And there was one of the officers, he was checking, you know, visitors camp. He met my husband, and he says-- start asking him questions, from where you come, where you-- what's your plans. He says, my plan's to go only to Palestine. And ask him if he have any relatives in America. He said, yes. I have a brother in Chicago. And that's all. He said, give me the name of your brother and your name and the city and-- and your brother will find you. He said my-- his father-in-law works at the [? Fulwitz ?] in New York, and I'll put us in the [? Fulwitz. ?] It's a paper, [? Fulwitz ?], a Yiddish, Jewish paper. You know. And then it happened. And after two weeks, he sent in papers with the picture of my husband and-- and the baby and everything. So his brother find us through the papers. Right away he send us papers to fill out and-- and we refused to-- to go-- to come to America. Just into-- to go to Palestine, we didn't have the certificate. So the-- the brother-in-law says, come to America. From America, you can go to Palestine. So we listened to him, and we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- what kind of passport did you use to get out of Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not have any passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no passport. Wait a minute. We have already Russian passport. We have already Russian passport. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went from Rowne to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Rowne, we went to Germany, Boynton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then to Bindermichl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Bindermichl we stayed--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: --till I told you that the officer find my husband, and he connected to-- to-- you know, through the [? Fulwitz ?] to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all well and healthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't-- I didn't feel good. I was laying more than-- it was-- I-- I got an-- a nervous heart, still up to date. Before I didn't feel I want to call you not to come. I didn't feel good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to-- where in the United States did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right-- New York. And [? Hyas ?] took us out. And we stay overnight in New York, then we came to Chicago by train to our brother-- to my husband's brother and sister-in-law. And we stayed there, then we couldn't find a room even that time. It was hard to find an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. So we visit our-- we have relatives in-- in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Chicago?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it Milwaukee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milwaukee. Yeah. So we visit Milwaukee, the relatives, and there we start looking for apartment in Milwaukee. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack was the time when we traveled, six months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your husband start to do in Milwaukee?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Milwaukee, he started-- he-- he worked for a-- he was a cutter for glove, by hand. Very hard work. Then he quit this job, was too hard. He used to come with blisters on his hand. And then-- then he worked for a shoe company.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
